web.markdown:
![Thumbnail (1920x1080)](https://i.ytimg.com/vi/hbdopoL8_KE/maxresdefault.jpg)
# [How To Fix Your Lava Lamp! Step-by-Step Guide to Replacing Wax & Fluid (Lava Lab Creations)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hbdopoL8_KE&vl=en)

**Visibility**: Public
**Uploaded by**: [Misfit Printing](https://www.youtube.com/@misfitprinting)
**Uploaded at**: 2025-08-25
**Published at**: 
**Length**: 10:24
**Views**: 41179
**Likes**: 1189
**Category**: Howto & Style

## Description

```
Use code MISFIT10 for 10% OFF your order at Lava Lab Creations! 
Shop Lava Lab Creations Here 👉 https://lavalabcreations.com/

In this video, learn how to completely refurbish your malfunctioning lava lamp. We’ll guide you from disassembly to cleaning, replacing the internal materials, and reassembling using supplies from Lava Lab Creations. This comprehensive video includes important safety tips, a detailed buyer's guide on choosing the right wax, and practical advice for achieving the best flow in your lamp. Big thanks to Lava Lab Creations for sponsoring and providing all the materials needed for this demonstration!

Lava Lamp Blog: https://misfitmeta.com/how-to-fix-lava-lamp/
Follow for short form on Tiktok:  https://www.tiktok.com/@misfit_printing


👹 For all things Misfit visit https://misfitmeta.com/ 👹


00:00 Introduction and Overview
00:15 Lava Lab Creations
00:40 Initial Steps: Unplugging and Cooling Your Lava Lamp
00:49 Removing the Lava Lamp Cap and Liquid
01:34 Melting and Removing the Lava Lamp Wax
03:43 Cleaning the Lava Lamp Globe
04:55 Choosing and Melting the New Wax For Your Lava Lamp
07:23 Pouring and Coloring the Lava Lamp Wax
08:41 Final Assembly and Testing Your Refurbished Lava Lamp
10:03 Conclusion
```

## Transcript

You have a lava lamp and it's not
working the way that you want to.
Chances are you probably need to replace
the stuff inside of it. Today I'm going
to show you from start to finish how to
take your lava lamp apart, how to get
the fluid out from inside, what
materials you need to replace everything
inside of your lava lamp, and how to fix
it once you have everything cleaned out.
With that said, today's video is
sponsored by Laval Lab Creations. Laval
Lab Creations is a company that I have
used all of the supplies from to replace
all the internals on the limbs I've dug
in my short form content. They not only
carry things like waxes and surfactants
for the inside of your lamp, but a bunch
of other supplies like springs, dimmers,
and so much more for other parts on your
lava lamp if you need them. We'll be
using their chemicals and instruction
for this demonstration today. And
hopefully, if you bought their supplies,
this video can be a helpful resource for
you, too. Kicking things off, if your
lava lamp is currently plugged in and
running, you're going to want to unplug
it and wait until your lamp is fully
cooled down before you start the removal
process. From there, take the lava lamp
off the base. And once you remove the
cap, you should see a second cap on the
inside. Most common cap that I see
inside the lava lamp is a traditional
bottle cap, but you may also see
something that's more similar to a cork
or a metal cap that you have to screw
off. Once you remove it, if it's still
in good shape, then set it to the side
and you can use it again to recap your
lava lamp at the end. However, if it's
damaged, you can use another bottle cap.
Or if you don't have any on hand, they
also have them at Lava Lab Creations.
From here, you can dump out the liquid
inside of your lamp and dispose of it.
or if you're an avid lava lamp collector
like me, maybe you set it aside and save
it for future projects. And just to
reiterate that we're on the same page,
the wax at the bottom of the lamp should
still be solid and should not pour out
with the liquid. This brings us to our
next step, which is melting the wax
down. I have a dedicated crafting pot
that I use for this, but you can really
use any pot, and you're going to fill it
up shallowly with water. Place the lava
lamp inside and bring it to a gentle
simmer. Before you do, this is a quick
safety check. be 1,000% certain that you
have removed the cap from the lava lamp
at this point. The reason being, if the
cap is still on the lava lamp, the
pressure will build up inside and it
will literally explode into a million
pieces. This obviously harms the lamp,
but can also result in bodily harm or
death if you are standing nearby. So
again, be 1,000% sure that you have
removed that cap. But back to rainbow
sunshine and lava lamps. usually takes
about 10 minutes for the wax to fully
melt inside of the lamp while it's
sitting on this boiler. This will really
be a theme throughout this entire
process that there is a lot of hurry up
and wait with fixing these lava lamps.
But just be patient and wait until it is
fully melted. Once the wax is ready to
be poured out, you're going to get a
separate dedicated container to pour it
into. What you do not want to do is pour
this down your drain unless you love
supporting your local plumber. Because
this is wax, once it cools back down, it
is going to resolidify. And although I
am no expert, I think this will probably
cause a problem. Now, keep in mind
because we have boiled this lava lamp
alive, it is going to be very hot. So,
use some kind of protection when you go
to pick it up. Unfortunately, I just
have oven mitts on hand. But best case
scenario, if you have those thermal
gloves with the little grippers on it,
that is the best thing you can use.
Again, because I do so many experiments
with my lava lamps, I like to save my
waxes for future projects. But if you're
just disposing of it, you can put it in
something less permanent than one of
these deli containers. In this case, I
did have a tiny chunk of wax that was
still sitting at the bottom of my lamp
that didn't melt. So, in this case, just
throw it right back on the boiler, let
it melt down, and then you can pour the
rust out. Once the majority of the wax
is out of the lamp, this is when we're
going to remove the spring down at the
bottom. You can tilt the lamp upside
down and pull it out with some kind of
stick or fork. Or if you have a set of
long forceps, you can grab it out with
those as well. In the off chance that
your lava lamp did not have a coil down
at the bottom already, they also have
those for sale lava lamp creations in
various different sizes. Either way,
give your coil a quick scrub dubdub and
then set it to the side for later. From
here, we're going to do the final clean
out of the globe of our lava lamp. Pour
hot water inside of the globe. And this
will help pull out any of those
remaining little bits of wax inside. I
just reuse the boiling water from my
pot. And at this point, depending on how
many wax remnants are left down at the
bottom, you could just dump it. However,
what I like to do is take a paper towel,
roll it up, stick it into the lamp like
some sort of Molotov cocktail. And from
there, all those little wax bits will
stick onto the paper towel and you can
just pull it out and dump out the water
from there. Once all the big particles
are gone, chances are your globe is
still going to be a little bit dirty.
This is especially true if you have a
really old lava lamp or if you were
running your lava lamp super frequently
and maybe some of the wax burnt onto the
side. I'll be using the globe cleaner
from Lava Lab Creations. But if you
didn't get the kit that includes it, you
can also use Dawn dish soap and then
scrub it out with a bottle cleaning
brush. Over the years of doing these
replacements, some of my 420 friendly
followers have also given me other
advice that they use to clean their
glass pieces, like shaking up water and
rock salt inside of the bottles. If you
do opt to do this method, just make sure
that you rinse it out really, really
well so that there's no salt remaining
in the bottle. With that said, whatever
method you choose, just repeat it a few
times until you're finally satisfied
with the result. Now, real quick, before
we get into melting down the wax, a
buyer's guide on what you're going to
want to look for when you're on the
Level Lab Creations website. Whether
you're looking at just the individual
wax or the full-on kits, there's going
to be different types of wax, and it's
really important to make sure that
you're buying the right one for the lamp
that you're trying to restore
specifically. Most common wax used for
most traditional lamps is going to be
the wax for 20 to 52 ouncez lamps. But
you'll also see options for grande
lamps, which are the really, really big
ones, as well as options for tower
lamps, which are the really tall and
thin ones. Because wax inside lamps
flows due to the density changes at the
top and the bottom temperature of the
water. If you don't buy the right wax,
it's not going to flow properly. So,
just make sure that you're paying extra
close attention for this step. Now, of
course, when the wax comes, it's going
to arrive solid. So, we have to melt it
down to get it inside of our lamp.
Although, I know it is tempting, do not
put this bottle of wax inside the
microwave. It will not heat properly and
it is potentially going to separate the
ingredients into parts that it
shouldn't. Instead, you're going to get
that handy dandy crafting pot back out.
And just like before, you're going to
fill it up with a little water and then
place the container of wax inside again
with the cap off. Now, the first time I
did this, it felt really encounterive
that I was going to place a plastic
bottle inside of a pot of boiling water.
But this bottle is meant to withstand
these temperatures, and it is not going
to melt on you, just the wax inside.
Again, this step is very much hurry up
and wait. It's going to take quite some
time to fully melt down, especially if
this is a fully new bottle of wax. Just
be patient and wait until it is fully
melted so that everything is blended
properly. Once your wax is fully melted,
these next few steps are all going to
happen very quickly at once. First,
you're going to coat the inside of your
globe with surfactant. Now, if you
bought one of the preassembled kits,
what you're going to want to be careful
of is make sure that you are grabbing
the surfactant specifically and not the
magic surf. From here, I put 1 milll of
surf and 1 milll of distilled water
inside the globe. Now, this is not a
martini, so swirl. Do not shake the
globe and coat the entire inside of the
globe with this mixture. Dump out any
extra and then set the globe to the
side. On the instructions included,
there will be a sizing guide for how
much wax you're going to allocate for
each different size of lava lamp that
you're filling. In this case, I'm doing
a 20 oz 14.5 in lamp. So, we're going to
allocate about 150 ml of wax.
Personally, I like to pour just over the
150ml line in this case because I know
I'm going to lose a little bit of wax
pouring between the two apparatuses and
I just like the look of a little bit
fuller lamp. Once your wax is measured
out into a heats safe measuring cup,
this is when you're going to dye the
wax. In this case, I opted to use the
teal dye. And I find that these wax dyes
go a long way. So, start with a little
bit to start and then you can always add
more if you need it. Once you think that
you're at the desired color, hold your
wax up to the sunlight. And if it looks
right when you're holding it up to the
light, that will be the right color
inside the lamp. Also, if you're having
any issues with getting the wax dye to
melt fully into the wax, you can always
put it back on the stove. From here,
you're going to put the funnel inside of
your lava lamp. And then you're going to
pour the wax into the lamp with
dedication. Basically, just pour it in
deliberately, carefully, but quickly.
The wax can smell your fear if you're
moving too slow, and it will drip down
the side and cause a mess. In my
excitement of making this video, I
almost forgot this part. But while your
wax is freshly put into the lamp, this
is when you're going to pop the coil
back inside. Finally, you'll put your
lava lamp with the wax inside back into
your boiling pot one last time to help
boil out any of the air bubbles that got
introduced during the pouring process.
From here, we will once again be waiting
on our lamp until the wax is fully
solidified in the bottom. Although,
we're getting close. And it is so
tempting to pour the liquid inside at
this point. If you do, it's going to mix
with the wax and then you're going to
have to start this whole process over.
Also, again, while it may be tempting,
don't do something crazy like put the
wax in the refrigerator or the freezer.
The quick temperature change could cause
the glass to shatter. In my experience,
this takes anywhere from 1 2 3ish hours,
just depending on how cold your house is
and how big the lamp is. Finally, once
the wax is fully cooled down inside the
lamp to the point where you can turn it
upside down and it does not fall out,
this is when we are going to pour in our
distilled water. Again, this wax is
specifically formulated to work with
distilled water, so it's important that
you use that specifically and not just
tap water. I pick up these gallons of it
from my grocery store, and they're
literally a dollar. I use my same funnel
to fill up the globe. And you're going
to want to make sure that you're leaving
about 2 in of space at the top of the
lamp for thermal expansion. Finally, my
absolute favorite part of this process,
adding the water dye. Just like
everything else, I got these dyes
specifically at Lava Lab Creations. They
are incredibly pigmented and they will
not mix in with your wax. And I just add
it drop by drop until I finally get the
desired color. From here, all that is
left to do is reseal the bottle cap on
the top of the lamp. They do sell some
dedicated cappers, but my most recent
method that I've been using is using
these hose clamps to tighten down the
cap on top of the lava lamp. However, if
you don't have any of those laying
around either, you can also just use the
bottle cap to crimp the cap back on into
place. Do note that this probably will
not create a watertight seal, but it
will be on there strong enough if you're
just running your lava lamp. From there,
all that's left to do is plug in your
lava lamp and let it run. I'm usually
pretty happy with how the lamp flows at
this point, but if you wanted to have
more flow to it, this is when you can
start adding your magic surf in small
increments. Again, big shout out to Lava
Lab Creations for providing all the
supplies in this video. Go check out
their website for all things Lava Lamp.
and let me know what other laval lamp
related videos you'd like to see us do

web.title: Lava lamp
web.description: Lava lamps consist of a bolus of wax and a clear or translucent liquid inside a glass vessel. Crestworth Ltd, based in Poole, Dorset. called the Lava Lite Lamp.
web.markdown:
# Lava lamp Lava lamp Decorative lamp [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Lava_lamps_%2816136876840%29.jpg/960px-Lava_lamps_%2816136876840%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Lava_lamps_(16136876840).jpg) Lava lamps[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/1990s_Mathmos_Astro.jpg/500px-1990s_Mathmos_Astro.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:1990s_Mathmos_Astro.jpg) An original [Mathmos](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mathmos "Mathmos") _Astro_ lava lamp A **lava lamp** is a decorative [lamp](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Light_fixture "Light fixture") that was invented in 1963 by British entrepreneur [Edward Craven Walker](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Edward_Craven_Walker "Edward Craven Walker"), the founder of the lighting company [Mathmos](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mathmos "Mathmos"). Lava lamps consist of a [bolus](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/bolus "wikt:bolus") of wax and a clear or translucent liquid inside a glass vessel. The vessel is placed on a lamp base with an [incandescent light bulb](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Incandescent_light_bulb "Incandescent light bulb"). The heat causes temporary reductions in the wax's density and the liquid's surface tension. As the warmed wax rises through the liquid, it cools, loses its buoyancy, and falls back to the bottom of the vessel in a cycle that is visually suggestive of [pāhoehoe lava](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lava#P%C4%81hoehoe "Lava"). The lamps are designed in a variety of styles and colours. Lava lamps are commonly associated with [hippie](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hippie "Hippie"), [psychedelia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Psychedelia "Psychedelia"), and [cannabis cultures](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cannabis_culture "Cannabis culture").[\[1\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lava_lamp#cite_note-1)[\[2\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lava_lamp#cite_note-2) ## Operation [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Blue_Lava_lamp.JPG/500px-Blue_Lava_lamp.JPG)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Blue_Lava_lamp.JPG) A cyan lava lamp A classic lava lamp contains a standard incandescent or [halogen lamp](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Halogen_lamp "Halogen lamp") which heats a tall (often tapered) glass bottle. A formula from a 1968 US patent consisted of water and a transparent, [translucent](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Transparency_(optics) "Transparency (optics)"), or opaque mix of mineral oil, [paraffin wax](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Paraffin_wax "Paraffin wax"), and [carbon tetrachloride](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbon_tetrachloride "Carbon tetrachloride").[\[3\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lava_lamp#cite_note-pat156-3) The clear water or mineral oil can optionally be coloured with translucent [dyes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dye "Dye"). Common wax has a [density](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Density "Density") much lower than water and floats at any temperature. Carbon tetrachloride is denser than water (also [nonflammable](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nonflammable "Nonflammable") and [miscible](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Miscible "Miscible") with wax) and can be added to increase the wax density at room temperature. When heated, the wax expands, becomes less dense, and rises; as it cools, its density increases and it sinks.[\[3\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lava_lamp#cite_note-pat156-3) The wax also becomes fluid, causing blobs to ascend to the top of the lamp.[\[3\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lava_lamp#cite_note-pat156-3) A metallic wire coil in the bottle's base breaks the cooled blobs' [surface tension](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Surface_tension "Surface tension"), allowing them to merge and repeat the cycle. Video of an orange lava lamp in operation Since 1970, lava lamps made for the US market have not used carbon tetrachloride, the use of which was banned in the country that year due to toxicity.[\[4\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lava_lamp#cite_note-4) Haggerty, their current manufacturer, has stated that their current formulation is a [trade secret](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trade_secret "Trade secret").[\[5\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lava_lamp#cite_note-5) The underlying [fluid mechanics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fluid_mechanics "Fluid mechanics") phenomenon in lava lamps is a form of [Rayleigh–Taylor instability](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rayleigh%E2%80%93Taylor_instability "Rayleigh–Taylor instability"). Agitating the lamp once the wax has melted causes the two fluids to [emulsify](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emulsion "Emulsion"), resulting in a cloudy appearance. Some emulsification occurs during the lamp cycle; however, the wax reforms into a single mass upon cooling. Severe cases can require many heating-cooling cycles to clear.[\[6\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lava_lamp#cite_note-6) In 2015, a new design was introduced that uses [ferrofluid](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ferrofluid "Ferrofluid") in place of wax.[\[7\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lava_lamp#cite_note-7) ## History [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Lava_Lamp_Factory_Chicago_IL.jpg/960px-Lava_Lamp_Factory_Chicago_IL.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Lava_Lamp_Factory_Chicago_IL.jpg) Former Lava Lamp factory, at 1650 [Irving Park Road](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Illinois_Route_19 "Illinois Route 19"), [Chicago](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") British entrepreneur [Edward Craven Walker](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Edward_Craven_Walker "Edward Craven Walker") conceived the lava lamp after observing a homemade egg timer made from a cocktail shaker bubbling on a pub stove.[\[8\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lava_lamp#cite_note-8) This precursor was designed and patented [GBpatent 703924](https://worldwide.espacenet.com/textdoc?DB=EPODOC&IDX=GB703924) by Donald Dunnet.[\[9\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lava_lamp#cite_note-9) Crestworth later launched the first 'Astro' lava lamp in 1963.[\[10\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lava_lamp#cite_note-10) After the lamp's commercial launch, Craven Walker worked with British inventor David George Smith, who is listed as the inventor on [U.S. Patent 3,387,396](https://patents.google.com/patent/US3387396) for a 'Display Device,' filed in 1965, issued in 1968, and assigned to Craven Walker's company, Crestworth Ltd, based in [Poole, Dorset](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Poole "Poole"). In 1965, Adolph Wertheimer and Hy Spector were intrigued by Walker's product when they saw it at a German trade show. They bought the American rights and began the Lava Manufacturing Corporation in [Chicago](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") to sell what they called the Lava Lite Lamp. In the late 1970s, US rights were sold to Larry Haggerty, who created a subsidiary of his company, Haggerty Enterprises, called Lava World International, which produced American lava lamps for over 30 years.[\[11\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lava_lamp#cite_note-11) In 2003, American lava lamp maker Lava World International (formerly Lava-Simplex-Scribe Internationale) moved its production to China. In 2008, it was acquired by Talon Merchant Capital and its name was changed to Lava Lite LLC. As of 2016, lava lamps were being sold under Lifespan brands in North America.[\[12\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lava_lamp#cite_note-12) In 2018, the "Lava Lamp" brand was acquired by toy and gift maker Schylling Inc. of North Andover, MA.[\[13\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lava_lamp#cite_note-13) who continue to hold the US trademark rights to both the shape and name of LAVA lamp.[\[14\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lava_lamp#cite_note-14) When the American rights were sold, Craven Walker retained the rights for the rest of the world and took on two business partners in the late 1980s, [Cressida Granger](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cressida_Granger_(entrepreneur) "Cressida Granger (entrepreneur)") and David Mulley. They eventually took over the company and changed its name to [Mathmos](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mathmos "Mathmos") in 1992. Edward Craven Walker remained a consultant to them until his death in 2000. Mathmos continues to make Lava Lamps and related products. They have won two Queens Awards for Export, and the Best Multi-Media prize at the Design Week awards.[\[15\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lava_lamp#cite_note-15)[\[16\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lava_lamp#cite_note-Bachelor-16) Astro lava lamp was launched in 1963 and celebrated its 50th anniversary in 2013.[\[17\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lava_lamp#cite_note-17) Mathmos lava lamps have been made in [Poole](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Poole "Poole"), [Dorset](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dorset "Dorset"), since 1963. ## See also - [Bubble light](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bubble_light "Bubble light")– Bubbling decorative light - [Lavarand](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lavarand "Lavarand")– Random number generator - [Liquid light show](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Liquid_light_show "Liquid light show")– Psychedelic art form - [Plasma globe](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plasma_globe "Plasma globe")– Decorative electrical device ## References 01. metadata.ogDescription: A lava lamp is a decorative lamp that was invented in 1963 by British entrepreneur Edward Craven Walker, the founder of the lighting company Mathmos. Lava lamps consist of a bolus of wax and a clear or translucent liquid inside a glass vessel. The vessel is placed on a lamp base with an incandescent light bulb. The heat causes temporary reductions in the wax's density and the liquid's surface tension. As the warmed wax rises through the liquid, it cools, loses its buoyancy, and falls back to the bottom of the vessel in a cycle that is visually suggestive of pāhoehoe lava. The lamps are designed in a variety of styles and colours. Lava lamps are commonly associated with hippie, psychedelia, and cannabis cultures.
